claims.claim_text: Coroot provides eBPF-based service-map discovery, SLO tracking, and continuous profiling for Kubernetes workloads.
claims.rationale: The Coroot GitHub README explicitly lists zero-instrumentation observability, service-map coverage, SLO tracking, and profiling as product features.

claims.claim_text: The module claims 23 Node.js services each required 4 hours of instrumentation and $13,800 of engineer cost.
claims.anchor_text: | Node.js services | 23 | 4 hours each | $13,800 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Example Node.js services | Several | Several hours each | Meaningful engineering cost |
claims.rationale: This is anonymous anecdotal math with precise counts and costs but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims 18 Python services each required 6 hours of instrumentation and $16,200 of engineer cost.
claims.anchor_text: | Python services | 18 | 6 hours each | $16,200 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Example Python services | Several | Several hours each | Meaningful engineering cost |
claims.rationale: This is anonymous anecdotal math with precise counts and costs but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims 8 Go services each required 5 hours of instrumentation and $6,000 of engineer cost.
claims.anchor_text: | Go services | 8 | 5 hours each | $6,000 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Example Go services | Several | Several hours each | Meaningful engineering cost |
claims.rationale: This is anonymous anecdotal math with precise counts and costs but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims 4 legacy Java services each required 12 hours of instrumentation and $7,200 of engineer cost.
claims.anchor_text: | Legacy Java | 4 | 12 hours each | $7,200 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | Example legacy Java services | A few | Longer per service | Meaningful engineering cost |
claims.rationale: This is anonymous anecdotal math with precise counts and costs but no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a total of 53 services, 276 hours, and $43,200 of instrumentation effort.
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Illustrative total** | **Dozens** | **Substantial engineering time** | **Substantial engineering cost** |
claims.rationale: The exact total is presented as fact but is unsupported anonymous anecdotal arithmetic.

claims.claim_text: Two full-time engineers supposedly spent 40% of their time maintaining observability code.
claims.anchor_text: Two full-time engineers spent 40% of their time maintaining observability code—not application features.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Teams can end up spending a noticeable share of engineering time maintaining observability code instead of application features.
claims.rationale: This is an uncited staffing statistic attached to the same unattributed anecdote.

claims.claim_text: A production outage allegedly lasted 47 minutes because tracing ended at an uninstrumented legacy Java service.
claims.anchor_text: A production outage lasted 47 minutes because traces ended at a legacy Java service with no instrumentation.
claims.suggested_rewrite: An outage can last longer when trace coverage ends at a poorly observed dependency or legacy service.
claims.rationale: This is a specific incident claim with exact duration and failure mode but no source.

claims.claim_text: The outage supposedly caused $127,000 in SLA credits.
claims.anchor_text: Post-mortem cost: $127,000 in SLA credits.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The outage led to meaningful contractual and operational cost.
claims.rationale: This is false precision about financial impact with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Coroot eliminates the instrumentation burden.
claims.anchor_text: **Coroot eliminates the instrumentation burden.**
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Coroot can reduce the amount of manual instrumentation needed to get useful observability data.**
claims.rationale: This absolute marketing claim is too strong given Coroot's own docs still distinguish eBPF spans from fully instrumented traces.

claims.claim_text: Coroot automatically discovers services, tracks dependencies, monitors SLOs, and provides distributed tracing without any instrumentation code.
claims.anchor_text: Using eBPF to observe applications at the kernel level, Coroot automatically discovers services, tracks their dependencies, monitors SLOs, and provides distributed tracing—all without a single line of instrumentation code.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Using eBPF and its own agents, Coroot can automatically discover services, map dependencies, and start collecting useful observability data quickly, while full-fidelity tracing still benefits from explicit instrumentation in some cases.
claims.rationale: Coroot's official tracing docs say eBPF spans are valuable for uninstrumented services but may not provide complete traces.

claims.claim_text: An e-commerce company allegedly had all 53 services visible, SLOs calculated, and traces flowing through a legacy monolith after only 2 hours of Helm work.
claims.anchor_text: That e-commerce company deployed Coroot on a Friday afternoon. By Monday morning: all 53 services visible, service map auto-generated, SLOs calculated, traces flowing through the legacy Java monolith that had been a black box for two years. Time invested: 2 hours of Helm commands. Not 276 hours of SDK integration.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team can deploy Coroot and quickly get broader visibility into previously hard-to-observe services without spending weeks on per-service SDK work.
claims.rationale: This is another unattributed success story with precise timing and outcome claims.

claims.claim_text: A fintech supposedly saved $380,000 annually by replacing Datadog with Coroot, dropping from $31K/month to about $800/month.
claims.anchor_text: - **One fintech saved $380,000 annually by replacing Datadog with Coroot** — Their 200-service deployment cost $31K/month in APM fees. Coroot (open source) plus ClickHouse hosting: ~$800/month. They got better visibility into legacy systems that Datadog's agents couldn't instrument.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Replacing a commercial APM with a self-hosted stack can reduce spend substantially** — For some teams, open-source tooling plus storage can cost far less than per-host or per-service APM pricing, especially at larger scale.
claims.rationale: This is a precise cost-savings case study with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Coroot supposedly detected a $2.1M incident in 3 minutes that traditional APM missed at a cryptocurrency exchange.
claims.anchor_text: - **Coroot detected a $2.1M incident in 3 minutes—traditional APM missed it entirely** — A cryptocurrency exchange experienced a trading halt from TCP retransmissions between their matching engine and database. Application metrics showed nothing wrong. Coroot's kernel-level TCP monitoring caught the network degradation immediately.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Kernel-level network telemetry can reveal incidents that application metrics miss** — TCP retransmissions and other transport issues can be invisible at the application layer until they impact user-facing behavior.
claims.rationale: This is a specific unsourced incident narrative with exact financial impact and response timing.

claims.claim_text: Zero-instrumentation tracing supposedly saves 2-4 weeks or 40-80 engineering hours per microservice.
claims.anchor_text: - **Zero-instrumentation tracing saves 2-4 weeks per microservice** — Traditional distributed tracing requires SDK integration, context propagation code, and careful testing. Companies report 40-80 hours of engineering time per service. Coroot captures trace context at the kernel level—instant tracing for any application, any language.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Manual distributed tracing rollout can add meaningful engineering work per service** — SDK integration, context propagation, and testing all take effort, whereas Coroot can provide immediate visibility for some traffic without per-service instrumentation.
claims.rationale: The specific time and hour estimates are presented as fact but have no cited source.

claims.claim_text: eBPF profiling supposedly found a $450K memory leak that Datadog and New Relic could not detect.
claims.anchor_text: - **eBPF profiling found a $450K memory leak invisible to APM** — A SaaS company's Go service had a native memory leak in a C library (CGO). Application heap metrics were stable, but container memory grew until OOMKill. Coroot's continuous profiling saw the off-heap growth that Datadog and New Relic couldn't detect.
claims.suggested_rewrite: - **Container-level telemetry can expose memory growth that application-reported heap metrics miss** — Native allocations and other off-heap behavior may not be obvious from application metrics alone.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced anecdote with precise financial impact and comparative vendor claims.

claims.claim_text: coroot-node-agent is a DaemonSet that runs on every node and uses eBPF to capture telemetry from containers.
claims.rationale: Coroot's official architecture docs appear to be the best primary source for the DaemonSet deployment model, but docs.coroot.com is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Coroot uses ClickHouse for logs, traces, and profiles, and Prometheus is optional because metrics can also be stored in ClickHouse.
claims.rationale: The ClickHouse configuration docs explicitly describe ClickHouse as storing logs, traces, profiles, and optionally metrics, but the official page is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Coroot discovers services by observing actual network traffic.
claims.rationale: The node-agent README says it traces TCP events to obtain an actual map of inter-service communications.

claims.claim_text: Coroot calculates availability and latency SLOs for every service without manual configuration.
claims.rationale: Coroot's SLO docs describe predefined availability and latency SLOs for every application, but the best source is on docs.coroot.com, which is off-allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Coroot captures distributed traces without any SDK.
claims.anchor_text: Coroot captures distributed traces without any SDK:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Coroot can generate useful eBPF-based spans for some uninstrumented services, but complete end-to-end traces are still best with explicit instrumentation:
claims.rationale: Coroot's own tracing docs say eBPF-based tracing is helpful for uninstrumented services but may not provide complete traces.

claims.claim_text: Coroot provides built-in CPU and memory profiling without code changes.
claims.anchor_text: Built-in CPU and memory profiling without code changes:
claims.suggested_rewrite: Continuous profiling is available, with eBPF-based CPU profiling and additional language-specific profilers for some memory-related views:
claims.rationale: Coroot's profiling docs distinguish eBPF CPU profiling from language-specific approaches used for other profile types.

claims.claim_text: Coroot exposes TCP-level network details such as connection errors and latency that application metrics may miss.
claims.rationale: The node-agent README explicitly lists connection errors and network latency measurements from traced TCP events.

claims.claim_text: Coroot correlates logs with metrics and traces.
claims.rationale: The official logs overview directly states that Coroot correlates logs with traces, metrics, and profiles, but it is off the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: A fintech supposedly had periodic OOMKills in a payment service after hours of runtime.
claims.anchor_text: A fintech company was experiencing periodic OOMKills in their payment processing service. The service would run fine for hours, then suddenly get killed by Kubernetes.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A service can show stable behavior for a while and then still get OOM-killed if container memory keeps rising for reasons the application's own metrics do not expose.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident anecdote presented as a real case.

claims.claim_text: Coroot supposedly showed that a Go service using CGO had a leaking C encryption library that never freed allocations.
claims.anchor_text: Coroot's continuous profiling showed that the Go service was using CGO to call a C library for encryption. The C library had a memory leak—it allocated buffers that were never freed.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Continuous profiling can help narrow down whether memory growth is coming from outside the application's own heap accounting.
claims.rationale: This is a highly specific unsourced diagnosis narrative.

claims.claim_text: Updating the crypto library allegedly fixed the leak.
claims.anchor_text: **The Fix**: Updated the crypto library to the latest version which fixed the leak.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The Fix**: The underlying issue was resolved only after the leaking dependency or component was identified.
claims.rationale: This is a specific remediation claim with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims OOMKill incidents dropped from 12 per month to zero.
claims.anchor_text: | **OOMKill incidents/month** | 12 | 0 | -100% |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **OOMKill incidents/month** | Recurring | Reduced markedly | Improved stability |
claims.rationale: This table row presents precise operational results without a source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims average incident cost was $8K each, or $96,000 per year before Coroot.
claims.anchor_text: | **Incident cost (avg $8K each)** | $96,000/year | $0 | $96,000 saved |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Incident cost** | Significant | Reduced | Lower incident spend |
claims.rationale: This is precise financial impact data with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims investigation time fell from 6 hours per incident across 12 incidents to a one-time 2-hour effort.
claims.anchor_text: | **Engineering time investigating** | 6 hrs/incident × 12 | 2 hrs (one-time) | $10,800 saved |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Engineering time investigating** | Repeated incident work | Mostly one-off diagnosis | Lower investigation effort |
claims.rationale: This row presents unsupported exact time savings and derived dollar savings.

claims.claim_text: The module claims customer churn fell from 3% to 0.5%, preserving $42,000 ARR.
claims.anchor_text: | **Customer churn from instability** | 3%/year | 0.5%/year | $42,000 ARR saved |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Customer impact from instability** | Noticeable | Reduced | Better reliability |
claims.rationale: This is unsupported business-impact precision.

claims.claim_text: The module claims the prior APM license cost $18,000 per year and provided no visibility into the issue.
claims.anchor_text: | **APM license (couldn't see issue)** | $18,000/year | $0 (open source) | $18,000 saved |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **APM license** | Commercial spend | Open-source software | Lower software licensing cost |
claims.rationale: This is a precise cost comparison tied to an unsourced case study.

claims.claim_text: The module claims ClickHouse hosting cost $2,400 per year.
claims.anchor_text: | **ClickHouse hosting** | $0 | $2,400/year | -$2,400 |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Storage/hosting** | Minimal | Additional self-hosted storage cost | Tradeoff for self-hosting |
claims.rationale: This is precise hosting-cost data with no attributable source.

claims.claim_text: The module claims a total annual impact of $164,400.
claims.anchor_text: | **Total Annual Impact** | | | **$164,400** |
claims.suggested_rewrite: | **Total Annual Impact** | | | **Material savings are possible, but workload-specific** |
claims.rationale: The bottom-line savings figure is unsupported false precision.

claims.claim_text: A CTO allegedly said the company paid $18,000 per year for an APM that could not see the bug, while Coroot found it in 15 minutes after 8 months of OOMKills.
claims.anchor_text: The CTO's post-mortem summary: "We paid $18,000/year for an APM that literally couldn't see this bug. Coroot—which is free—found it in 15 minutes. The memory leak had been causing OOMKills for 8 months."
claims.suggested_rewrite: The team concluded that their previous tooling did not make the bug obvious, while Coroot surfaced the problem quickly once deployed.
claims.rationale: This is an unsourced verbatim quote plus multiple precise duration and cost claims.

claims.claim_text: Application-level metrics only showed the Go heap, while Coroot's eBPF profiling supposedly saw full container memory including native allocations, and traditional APM would have missed the issue entirely.
claims.anchor_text: **The Lesson**: Application-level metrics only showed the Go heap. Coroot's eBPF-based profiling saw the whole container memory, including native allocations. Traditional APM would have missed this entirely.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **The Lesson**: Application-level metrics can miss memory growth outside an application's own heap accounting; container-level telemetry and profiling data can help expose those cases.
claims.rationale: The first clause may be a reasonable lesson, but the specific Coroot/APM contrast is overstated and not backed by an attributable source here.

further_reading.title: Coroot GitHub README
further_reading.why_relevant: Best allowlisted high-level source for Coroot's zero-instrumentation positioning, service map, SLO tracking, tracing, and profiling features.

further_reading.title: coroot-node-agent README
further_reading.why_relevant: Best allowlisted source for node-agent kernel requirements, TCP tracing, service-map generation, log discovery, and OOM-related telemetry.

further_reading.title: Prometheus Overview
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful background for understanding how Coroot relates to Prometheus-style metrics pipelines and scraping.

further_reading.title: OpenTelemetry Traces
further_reading.why_relevant: Useful baseline for comparing Coroot's eBPF-derived spans with conventional distributed tracing instrumentation.

notes: Code blocks, quiz items, and hands-on exercise steps were intentionally excluded per the seed rules. Several exact Coroot product-behavior claims appear supportable from official docs on docs.coroot.com, but that domain is off the current allowlist, so those claims are marked needs_allowlist_expansion rather than forced onto weaker adjacent sources.
